title: Getting Help and Exploring R Environment
description: Learn how to access R's built-in help and manage your workspace.

slides.title: R's Built-in Help System 📚
slides.content:
R has extensive documentation. You can access it in several ways:

* `help(function)` or `?function` – Opens help page for a function.
* `help.search('topic')` or `??topic` – Searches for topics.
* `example(function)` – Runs examples from the help page.
* `vignette(package)` – Opens a long-form guide for a package.

Try `?mean` in the console.

slides.title: Using Help
slides.content: Run these commands to see help in action:

quizData.question: What is the quickest way to get help on the `plot` function in R?

slides.title: Vignettes: Long-form Documentation 📖
slides.content:
Packages often include vignettes – detailed tutorials. To see all vignettes for installed packages, use `vignette()`. To view a specific one:

```r
vignette('ggplot2-specs')
```

If the package isn't installed, you won't see its vignettes.

slides.title: Built-in Datasets 📊
slides.content:
R comes with many practice datasets. Use `data()` to see a list. Some popular ones:

* `iris` – Measurements of iris flowers.
* `mtcars` – Motor trend car road tests.
* `airquality` – Daily air quality measurements.

Load a dataset with `data(dataset_name)` and it appears in your environment.

slides.title: Exploring a Dataset
slides.content: Let's load and explore the `iris` dataset:
slides.codeSnippet:
data(iris)
head(iris)  # first 6 rows
str(iris)   # structure of dataset
summary(iris)  # summary statistics

slides.title: Useful Exploration Functions 🚀
slides.content:
* `str()` – compact display of object structure (type, dimensions, first few values).
* `summary()` – for numeric data gives min, max, quartiles; for factors gives counts.
* `head()` / `tail()` – first/last few rows.
* `dim()` – dimensions of data frame/matrix.
* `names()` – column/variable names.

These are your go-to tools for understanding any new dataset.

quizData.question: What information does `str(iris)` provide?

options.text: Summary statistics like mean and median
The structure of iris including data types and first values

quizData.explanation: `str()` shows the internal structure of an R object, including its type, dimensions, and a preview of the data.

slides.title: Workspace Management 💾
slides.content:
Your workspace (global environment) contains all the variables you've created. You can save it to a file and reload it later.

* `save.image(file = 'myworkspace.RData')` – Saves all objects.
* `load('myworkspace.RData')` – Loads saved objects.
* `save(object1, object2, file = 'myobjects.RData')` – Saves specific objects.

You can also use the Environment pane in RStudio to import/export.

slides.title: Save and Load Example
slides.codeSnippet:
x <- 1:10
y <- letters[1:5]
save.image('test.RData')  # saves everything
rm(list = ls())  # clear workspace
ls()  # empty
load('test.RData')
ls()  # x and y are back

slides.title: Summary
slides.content:
* Use `?` or `help()` to access documentation.
* Use `vignette()` for package tutorials.
* Practice with built-in datasets like `iris`.
* Explore data with `str()`, `summary()`, `head()`.
* Save and load your workspace with `save.image()` and `load()`.

Now you're ready to start your R journey!
